sentences.text: Yes.
Well, at first, we just had big fires.
And we all sat around them.
And we washed in the forest, you know, with the cold water-- whatever was available.
And we slept in our clothes.
We didn't undress.
We went to the toilet in the bushes.
There was no paper.
[LAUGHS] So we had to use leaves.
[LAUGHS] And we had food.
The men all had arms.
At this stage, the group was small.
So they went-- I remember on the first day, my husband went with two other men.
They took him right away and said, let's go and get some food in the villages.
He brought-- so they brought three men, they brought three chickens.
And I boiled them up.
I said, I have nothing to make chicken soup with.
You know, I haven't got vegetables, I haven't got-- I have a bit of salt.
I haven't got anything-- only a few potatoes.
I cooked the three chicken-- put them in a bucket with water and held it over the fire.
And cooked and put a few potatoes.
And the most delicious chicken soup you've ever had!
[LAUGHS] Hot, and--
And then I had the chicken.
And I said, what?
We're eating chicken and chicken soup in the forest?
So food was good.
Not-- we didn't have any fruit or vegetables or eggs or milk-- anything like this-- or butter.
But lots of meat, because this was the easiest thing to get.
And fat, and bread.
We had enough food-- at this stage, we had enough food.
We weren't short of food.
So the men used to go out in the-- to the villages and get some food.
And we're sitting for a little while in this base, and then we have to go to another base.
You want name the bases, or want the names of the places?
Well, this was--
We went to a-- a village called Chrapieniewo.
I don't know how to, uh-- you find-- C-H--- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] C-H-R-A-P-I-E-N-I-E-W-O.
I think it's-- Chrapieniewo.
And we stayed for a little while there.
And-- and also, made big fires in the forest, and we went to gather some food at night.
And slept around the fire.
One side would get very hot-- the other side would get frozen.
Have to change around, to sit with this side to the fire and then this side to the fire.
That's how we kept warm.
And then, all of a sudden, we had an attack, because some of the people didn't go into-- the safest place to survive was in the forest.
But some people tried to get them a little bit more comfortable, and they tried to sleep in trusted-- trusted people's houses around the forest.
In this case, it was the-- the-- the whole group of the Bielski family-- his wife, his sister-in-law, his sister-in-law's son-- a few-- a few.
Most of them, they didn't want to go to the forest.
Yeah.
And his sister and her nephew, Christian, a beautiful boy.
Used to sing so beautifully for us near the fire.
And somebody [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] alerted the police, and they ambushed and attacked in the thing, and they died.
This was the first time that somebody from that triad got killed.
And we decided to leave this place.
Before I came to that triad, they had built zemlyankas for winter-- prepared.
A zemlyanka is a place under-- under the ground. 'Zemlya' means 'earth' or 'land.' Earth.
So they dig sort of a-- what do you call it-- not a square, but a--
Rectangled places, deep into the forest-- about five foot or something deep, with a little bit outside.
You can-- you put the roof from the branches of the wood.
The whole walls were reinforced by branches from the wood.
We camouflaged it with things from-- branches from the trees.
And there was a little ladder to get in.
And there were two rows of banks on both sides of the walls.
In the middle, we got a stove with a pipe through the ceiling.
And we burned this-- in daytime we couldn't burn, because the smoke was the-- but at night, everything took place at night.
At day time, we were all huddle up-- everyone close to each other, and try to keep warm.
And at night, we did all the-- all the work, you know?
And the women were cooking.
I-- when I was cooking, I could steal a bit of hot water and get a wash.
We washed each other.
And we only went out in the thing for a little while, to catch some air-- fresh air.
And we looked at each other and laughed, because we were all yellow.
But it was warm, and it was cozy, and we had food-- the people.
So I had potatoes-- they dug holes.
This was all before I joined the partisans.
It was all prepared.
They had holes filled with potatoes, and then the men went and-- and got some other articles of food from the peasants, like some meat and some fat and mostly pork, you know?
This-- and-- we had enough food.
We could survive on this food.
We cooked in the big thing-- a big kettle of soup, with meat all together.
And we all ate it.
We weren't hungry.
We're light-- and we were very, you know-- locked in.
And this was very good for winter, because we got into-- this was coming December and everything.
So we decided to go into the winter zemlyankas.
I remember going on foot-- it was quite a fair bit from the last place to this place.
And we went into the zemlyankas, and we organized ourselves, and we stayed there for a little while.
And one day somebody-- and there was about four of them-- two in one place, and two in another place.
And one empty one, just in case somebody gets sick, you know-- to isolate him.
It was all prepared before I came to the triad, to the partisans.
And so we went into those zemlyankas the way and-- we arrived, and had a look, and we decided that's it, you know.We go in.
And we were there for about two months-- or six weeks or seven weeks, I can't remember-- from December to February.

And somebody went to-- to get some supplies, some food, and they killed-- I don't remember-- a chicken or a pig or something, and they didn't drain it from the blood, and they had the whole-- a whole-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prints-- the print-- what do you call it?
The-- The trail?
The trail, yeah.
The trail led right to our places.
And, not soon after, they attacked us-- the base.
The Germans.
Or the police-- the local police, you know.
They attacked us.
They followed the trail.
But we had good intelligence.
Good [NON-ENGLISH], they were called-- good people what watched.
It was two rows-- first was about a kilometer.
So there was time to tell us.
So we left the base in winter, and we tried to go to places where there was no footprints.
All those people knew the-- all the-- all the byways all the local people.
We got out in the middle of winter from our warm zemlyankas.
And went to another base.
And-- and you want me to name the place?
It was called [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was--
I wonder why they choose this place, because it was a forest, and every part of the forest was surrounded with little paths.
And we heard, all day, movements on the paths.
If somebody was to surround us, it would have been very easy to catch us.
I don't know why they picked that place, but it didn't last long.
We didn't stay long there.
We dug our wells.
You know how we-- you dug a well?
You came in, you tried, dig until you came to water.
And then the men made some-- with parts of the tree.
So sewed up, and they make, I don't know how it's called in English.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
And the water came up, and it was clean.
It cleaned itself through the thing.
And every zemlyanka had water.
Put in a bucket-- wells.
It was very-- at first I was amazed.
And then I got used to it, you know?
I mean, you come into the middle, you have a well.
And we got-- always got water.
And we didn't stay long in the [NON-ENGLISH].
We went to another base.
And the other one was a little bit more permanent.
Because it was summer, and it was, uh-- I don't know.
We didn't have any disturbance.
We were there about six weeks, I think, or something, which is a lot for a base.
We never were anywhere for this long.
And then one day my husband went on a mission.
And another group-- two groups went on missions.
And one group came right away, the same night.
They're supposed to come in a couple of days or something.
And the same night they returned.
They said, it's full of Germans in the thing-- on the ways.
A whole lot of-- And so I knew-- this particular moment, I had a feeling that my husband won't be returning.
And, sure enough, he didn't return.
There were eight-- eight of them.
And a few days after, they found the dead.
But my husband wasn't among the dead.
So they burned the village.
As I said, a few of them who managed to escape into the houses, the houses were burned.
They probably got burned inside the house.
I don't know-- so I they crossed them out as dead, you know-- the whole group.
He was absent for a month.
They were absent three men.
Five they buried, and three men were absent.
And we went to a-- we had to leave this base, which was a very nice base.
We had to leave it and went somewhere else-- another base.
And we stayed there for about three weeks.
And then my husband came, with the two other man.
They survived-- it's a long story.
I won't talk about it.
But they-- they couldn't reach the other triad, because it was-- the place it was thick with Germans.
And so they joined another Russian triad, for the time being, and they couldn't send any messages, because everything was in disarray.
It was streaming with Germans everywhere.
And so, when things were quieting down, they arrived.
And the day before, I knew that they were alive.
They send a message.
And they came, and I had my husband back again.
And then, after-- how much?
A week?
Not quite a week, or maybe a few days-- our base was attacked again.
When was this?
What month?
Yeah, this was in, I'll say-- let's see.
This was May, June, July-- probably June or July-- something like this.
It was warm.
It was summer.
We slept in a little tent made of a couple of peasant rags.
Yeah, this was 1943.
So it probably was in-- in May he disappeared, my husband.
This was in May, so in June he came.
So it was probably end of June or something like this.
Or beginning of July.
I don't know exactly.
And we got attacked again, very early in the morning.
And we all got up.
We all slept, of course, in our clothes.
Nobody ever took his-- and with the arms beside.
I didn't have any arms, but my husband did.
And the other couple who slept in the same hut, her husband has.
There were two couples in the same hut.
So the commandeer, like Mr.
Bielski, took us all out in one spot-- said to-- you know, we're being attacked, and we've got to divide our group in two parts-- one part for the-- with the arms, and one part without arms.
Because just in case we have to fight them off, we will protect the women and the people-- the unarmed people.
And they didn't attack, but we're running away, we didn't directly got attacked.
So they didn't have to fight.
But I was separated from my husband again.
[LAUGH] I didn't see him again for two weeks.
But by this time-- and sort of the-- everybody ran away.
We didn't want to run one group.
Everybody went.
I ended up with the group where the other Bielski brother was-- Zus-- with his group.
And, you know, everybody sort of divided in groups, and we kept running for our lives.
And the bullets were over our heads, and we heard the voices-- the German voices-- and everything.
So my husband ended up with one group, because he was-- and I ended up with the other group.
But, soon after, we found out that I'm safe, and I found out that he's safe.
So it was OK.
One day I knew that he was safe, and he knew that I was safe.
So we both were unharmed.
And at one stage, I was stuck in the mud.
I was running away.
I had something in my hand.
And I had some boots.
My husband was-- I was very lucky.
My husband managed, from all the boots, something to make order for me from a shoemaker who was there, a tradesman-- he made a pair of boots for me, and he got me some jodhpurs.
And I was very, well, elegantly dressed, for a partisan.
He was very resourceful.
And there was this other lady.
She was a doctor.
Tamara Zuskin.
And we were sort of together in the same group.
And she wore boots.
She was a big, tall woman.
I was a small woman, you know-- small.
And, at one stage, we were in one spot-- at the edge of a forest, and there was the other bit of forest where everybody already ran out, and we were behind.
And there was a little swamp between us.
And we had to cut this to get to the other-- there was no other way.
We were exposed.
And we kept waiting and waiting till, you know-- to make our decision to go into the swamp.
And then we heard the people behind-- the Germans behind us.
And they started to-- heard the voices.
We heard the-- the bullets over our heads.
So we said, we'll take it-- we'll cut it.
So we went into this little swamp.
And I couldn't-- look, our feet got stuck, the boots got stuck, and we had to run.
So first thing, I threw away what I had in my hand-- I never was holding onto my possessions.
Always, life came first, you know?
Possessions came later.
So I threw away my possession.
I couldn't get the boots out.
So-- and she couldn't get the boots out.
So we gave each other the hands.
I pulled her out, and she pulled me out, first, because she was stronger-- bigger.
And the pair of boots was stuck in the mud.
[LAUGHS]
And when we run back-- eventually we run to the edge of the forest.
So we could see-- and we fell on the ground.
And we could see actually the bullets going into the three trunks, just above our head.
We just made it.
But sometimes a pair of boots, you know, is a disadvantage, because I got stuck, and we couldn't pull our feet out.
Because it's hard to pull out-- feet out of boots.
But, you know, being two together, we helped each other, and we got it.
You know?
So this was this.
And then went to another base, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I met my husband.
We came all together, the whole group-- the whole Bielski triad came together.
We decided-- How many people were there, at this stage, about?
At this stage, I would say, we had a lot from Lida.
We had from [NON-ENGLISH], we had from-- maybe 500, or something like this, or 600.
I don't know.
It was at this time there were a lot of people escaping-- all the ghettos were being liquidated, and people escaping.
And they came from the Lipiczanska Pushcha.
A lot came from Lida.
Oh, I'm sitting [INAUDIBLE].
And--
No-- yes.
We moved more or less together.
Not-- you couldn't get such a big group together.
I was always marvelling-- how could you get away with it?
You know, we just walked a day at night and slept at-- hidden at day.
And there was no food.
Because in daylight, you couldn't go to the villages and get some food.
At nighttime, we're busy working.
So there was almost no food.
You know, I remember once taking-- and without knowing that I was already pregnant.
And when they asked the women and children for the food-- sick and unsick women and children could get some-- just a very little food.
I didn't have the courage to go, because I was sick and I was-- and I was pregnant.
[LAUGHS] So I took-- I was wearing a little jacket, so I put my hands in the pocket and overturned the pocket and looked for some crumbs.
Maybe something got into the pocket and tried to-- tried to-- I was so hungry!
Well, a lot of women.
At first not so many, but later on, when they start escaping, I'd say-- I couldn't really estimate.
Less than half, I would say.
Like, of the 1,200 that came-- eventually came out, I'll say 700 and 500 women and children.
Something like that.
Yeah, I managed.
So, by this time, when we came-- I wasn't yet sure.
Because I went to a doctor, and I told him that I'm-- I'm different.
I haven't got my period, and I hadn't, uh-- and when I go to the toilet, we-- at a lot of-- are coming out in worms.
I had a lot of worms.
Because we didn't eat a proper diet.
So he said-- he touched my breasts, he touched my-- he examined me a little bit.
He said, you know-- during the war, he wasn't a proper doctor-- Dr.-- I won't name.

And he said, during the war, a lot of women-- half of the women in the zemlyanka didn't have their periods, because emotional-- I lost my period, in '39, for a year-- when the bombing of Warsaw was going down.
For a year I didn't have any period.
But I wasn't worrying, because I didn't have anything to do with any man.
But he said, that's the wartime symptom.
We don't-- we can't-- tell yet.
It was an early stage.
You can't tell yet.
So I just let it go.
And then, when-- but that-- I'll come to that later.
So when we eventually decided that we're going to the pushcha.
There's no way we can-- we ran out of local bases, and the triad is getting big, and there's no room for-- no safety anymore in the-- in those-- in the district.
We've got to go on pushcha.
We're divided, of course, in groups, because you can't get so many people together.
But we follow each other-- followed each other.
And we had leaders who sort of led us.
And we came and crossed the Niemen.
On this big river that crosses the [NON-ENGLISH] Naliboki Pushcha.
And a lot of people could swim.
Some had-- some had the little canoes.
And-- but I went on a horseback.
Somebody could ride a horse, and there were two of us behind him.
And he went forwards and backwards and took everybody else across.
So eventually we came to the edge of the pushcha.
And we met, uh-- I won't talk about all those political things, with other partisans, with this-- that's not my story.
That's the-- the historia.
Were there other partisans living in the forest?
Yeah, it was teeming with partisans.
Just everybody came in, and it was full and full of partisans there.
It was a different world altogether, on the pushcha.
It was just like an army.
Naliboki.
Huge pushcha.
And all the Russian soldiers who couldn't get away when they were trapped-- most of them went to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, what do you call it?
War prisons-- became war prisoners.
And the ones that were more sensible went-- they had their arms and everything-- they went into the forest.
And they found themselves in the partisans.
They dominated the forest, of course.
They put conditions, and-- you know, you have to fight, you have to not just survive, but you have to fight.
And if you call yourself a partisan, you have to do things, you know?
They didn't accept children or women or unarmed people.
They were strictly military people.
So in this way, we were the only ones that accepted everybody who wanted to save themselves.
There was no conditions.
Everybody-- just like today, everybody and who wants-- who can't live in their country can go to Israel.
You know, that was like going to the Jewish state.
[LAUGHS] We felt safe there, yeah.
And when we came to the pushcha, I-- I-- a doctor came in.
And I was getting a bit-- I think-- I think a bit bigger.
And I went to this doctor.
She was a gynecologist.
And she examined me, and she said, you are fifth month pregnant.
She said, I can't perform an abortion-- because there were quite a few women, pregnant, that performed abortions.
I can't perform abortion.
You've got to wait till you're six months pregnant, and we can make, uh-- I think, a-- a premature [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] birth, sort of like.
Child who were born but not live.
Which a lot of people did.
You're going through the same pains and everything.
A lot of people did this, because this-- in spite of everything, you know?
I mean, people were people, and life was short, and people were coupling up together, you know, men and women.
They feel safer together.
And normal life took place.
So anyway, she said to me, well, we have to wait another month or one and a half months.
When the time comes, we'll perform this abortion on you.
So that was it.
And then came the big hunt.
Shortly afterwards came-- came the big hunt.
This was probably started in August, or something-- or in July.
After we came to-- to the pushcha.
The big hunt.
And--
I went through-- this was the most terrible thing that could happen.
You know, we were besieged.
Real-- three divisions, I think, they had.
A big [INAUDIBLE], because the-- there was-- '43, they were already losing in Stalingrad, and they were already losing the war.
But the partisans made such a damage to the German army.
Because they hijacked the food, and they did the-- pulled out the trains-- the rail trains, and they ambushed-- they could-- whatever they could do.
It was a whole army.
It was called the 'back of the army.' We were called the 'tail'-- the back.
So they had to do something about it.
So they did-- had three divisions with everything-- the most sophisticated arms and everything-- aeroplanes and tanks and everything.
And they just actually went into the forest after the-- after the partisans.
You know?
It was very dangerous.
And we didn't know what to do in such a big, big place.
And we had, of course, army gas-- because there were people that came from the Naliboki.
One particular man who was working as a-- a wood-- he was choosing wood for the-- for timber for the furniture.
There was a big trade-- to build a canal in which way-- because there was no trains there.
Before the war, that was a very big timber industry there.
And one of them worked, and another one also knew the area very good.
And we found that-- they came, and they were leading us into a spot in a valley, on a little hill-- not in a valley, on a hill-- where we had to-- might be safe a little while.
Because I don't think that the Germans will go through the swamp.
So about how many-- three, four days?
We went-- had to go through the swamp.
Imagine me, six month pregnant.
My seventh month.
After there in the swamp, we couldn't move, we couldn't go.
We didn't have any food.
We didn't have any water.
And every step was something-- you know?
You have to pull out everything you had in.
A few older carriage-- and all the energy and everything required.
That night-- she pulled something out.
Only the will to survival put me through it.
Many times I stopped and I told my husband not to-- to go ahead.
You know?
By then I had my two sisters with me.
And this is another story.
And we now, they all encouraged me to go on.
I didn't want to go-- to go on.
I was too tired.
I was just about dead.
And no food and no water, and mud, you know?
Every time, stepping up--
And we arrived eventually in this little hill, which was dry.
And we had some berries.
We picked some berries.
We got some water from somewhere. now, what we come back to life.
We rested there.
And the Germans were coming close, all the time.
We heard the voices.
We heard the thing-- the artillery bombing and everything.
But it's just like-- was like a cat and mouse.
I don't think they-- they-- didn't want to go around the camp to the swamps.
They stopped and went on a little bit, and they didn't go.
